what: 1. Run clustering analyses using map_bioreg() to map bioregions
title: Map bioregions
text: step translate site‐level ζ₂ predictions spatial bioregions. Calling map_bioreg() predictors_df following: z-scales predicted turnover, longitude latitude; fits four clustering algorithms (k-means, PAM, hierarchical GMM); k-means partitions points around centroids fast large data sets. PAM (Partitioning Around Medoids) medoid-based analogue k-means robust outliers. Hierarchical agglomerative clustering builds dendrogram “cuts” chosen k, capturing nested structure data. GMM (Gaussian Mixture Model) treats clusters multivariate normal distributions assigns point maximum likelihood. realigns method’s labels k-means solution consistency; builds nearest-neighbour thin-plate-spline interpolated surfaces; returns raw cluster assignments gridded rasters, —show_plot=TRUE—draws 2×2 panel maps. result set complementary bioregion maps rasters can use compare different algorithms partition landscape based compositional turnover geography.
code: # Add this to {, fig.width=11.25, fig.height=9, warning=FALSE, message=FALSE} # Run `map_bioreg` function to generate and plot clusters bioreg_current = dissmapr::map_bioreg(   data = predictors_df,   scale_cols = c("pred_zetaExp", "centroid_lon", "centroid_lat"),   method = 'all', # Options: c("kmeans","pam","hclust","gmm","all"),   k_override  = 8,   interpolate = 'nn', # Options: c("none","nn","tps","all"),   x_col ='centroid_lon',   y_col ='centroid_lat',   res = 0.5,    crs = "EPSG:4326",   plot = TRUE,   bndy_fc = rsa)  # Check results str(bioreg_current, max.level=1)

title: Map bioregions
text: expand workflow map forecasted ζ₂ bioregions three extreme climate futures (2030, 2040, 2050) alongside current scenario. see bioregional partitions shift, split all_preds scenario apply map_bioreg() (k-means + hierarchical, NN TPS interpolation). extract hierarchical cluster layers, mask study area, plot four maps 2×2 layout: visualise nearest-neighbour interpolated future‐scenario cluster outputs. First, list structure bioreg_future result confirm available components. combine k-means nearest-neighbour rasters “current” future year single SpatRaster stack (future_nn), resample, mask RSA boundary (mask_future_nn). Finally, lay 2×2 plot grid, compute discrete colour palette layer based unique classes, render masked layer boundary overlay quick inspection bioregion changes across time. end--end workflow shows predicted turnover patterns resulting bioregions might shift climate warms rainfall changes, highlighting potential future reorganization biodiversity hotspots.
code: # Split your combined predictions by scenario into a named list by_scn = split(all_preds, all_preds$scenario)  # For each scenario, call map_bioreg() with all algorithms bioreg_future = dissmapr::map_bioreg(   data = by_scn,   scale_cols = c("pred_zetaExp", "centroid_lon", "centroid_lat"),   method = 'all', # Options: c("kmeans","pam","hclust","gmm","all"),   k_override  = 8,   interpolate = 'nn', # Options: c("none","nn","tps","all"),   x_col ='centroid_lon',   y_col ='centroid_lat',   res = 0.5,    crs = "EPSG:4326",   plot = TRUE,   bndy_fc = rsa)  # Check results str(bioreg_future, max.level=1) # Check results str(bioreg_future, max.level=1)  # Create SpatRast future_nn = c(bioreg_future$nn$current$kmeans_current,              bioreg_future$nn$`2030`$kmeans_2030,              bioreg_future$nn$`2040`$kmeans_2040,              bioreg_future$nn$`2050`$kmeans_2050) names(future_nn)  # 4) Mask `result_bioregDiff` to the RSA boundary mask_future_nn = terra::mask(resample(future_nn, grid_masked, method = "mod"), grid_masked)  # 5) Quick visual QC in a 2×2 layout old_par = par(mfrow = c(2, 2), mar = c(1, 1, 1, 5)) titles = c("Current",             "2030",             "2040",             "2050")  for (i in 1:4) {   ## 1. how many distinct classes in this layer?   cls  = sort(unique(values(mask_future_nn[[i]])))   cls  = cls[!is.na(cls)]   n    = length(cls)    ## 2. build a discrete palette of n colours   pal = if (n <= 12) {            RColorBrewer::brewer.pal(n, "Set3")                      # native Set3          } else {            colorRampPalette(brewer.pal(12, "Set3"))(n) # extended Set3          }    ## 3. plot   plot(mask_future_nn[[i]],        col      = pal,        type     = "classes",          # treats values as categories        colNA    = NA,        axes     = FALSE,        legend   = TRUE,        main     = titles[i],        cex.main = 0.8)    plot(terra::vect(rsa), add = TRUE, border = "black", lwd = .4) }  par(old_par)

what: 1. Map sensitivity of bioregion delineation to clustering method using map_bioreg_diff()
title: Map bioregion change
text: sections use map_bioreg_diff() assess much four clustering algorithms disagree (sensitivity check). treat various cluster maps generated map_bioreg() (k-means, PAM, hierarchical GMM) sensitivity analysis. feeding four algorithm outputs map_bioreg_diff(), quantify much methods disagree. shows areas robust algorithm choice method‐dependent. Change-metric options map_bioreg_diff() include (approach argument): difference_count: counts many times cell’s label deviates first layer. shannon_entropy: Shannon entropy label sequence, measure within-cell diversity. stability: proportion layers label unchanged (1 = always stable, 0 = always different). transition_frequency: total number label flips consecutive layers, showing often change occurs. weighted_change_index: cumulative change weighted dissimilarity matrix rare large transitions score higher. (default): returns five-layer SpatRaster containing every metric.
code: # Get current nn rasters current_nn = c(bioreg_current$nn$current$kmeans_algn_current,              bioreg_current$nn$current$pam_algn_current,              bioreg_current$nn$current$hclust_algn_current,              bioreg_current$nn$current$gmm_algn_current) names(current_nn)  # Run `map_bioreg_diff` # 'approach', specifies which metric to compute: sens_bioregDiff = dissmapr::map_bioreg_diff(   current_nn,   approach = "all" )  # Inspect the output layers sens_bioregDiff  # Crop to our study area and prepare for plotting mask_sens_bioregDiff = terra::mask(   terra::resample(sens_bioregDiff, grid_masked, method = "near"),   grid_masked )  # Quick visual QC in a 3×2 layout old_par = par(mfrow = c(3, 2), mar = c(1, 1, 1, 5)) titles = c("Difference count", "Shannon entropy", "Stability",            "Transition frequency", "Weighted change index")  for (i in seq_along(titles)) {   plot(mask_sens_bioregDiff[[i]],        col      = viridis(100, direction = -1),        colNA    = NA,        axes     = FALSE,        main     = titles[i],        cex.main = 0.8)   plot(terra::vect(rsa), add = TRUE, border = "black", lwd = 0.4) } par(old_par)

what: 2. Map bioregion sensitivity to future change using map_bioreg_diff()
title: Map bioregion change
text: use map_bioreg_diff() track hierarchical‐cluster map changes three future climate scenarios. Focusing solely hierarchical solution map bioregion change across time. First stack hierarchical clusters today, 2030, 2040 2050, run map_bioreg_diff() series, highlight bioregions shift future climate projections. way isolate climate-driven reorganization hierarchical map .
code: # 1. Build a multi‐layer SpatRaster of hierarchical clusters for each scenario # Create SpatRast future_hclt = c(bioreg_future$nn$current$hclust_current,              bioreg_future$nn$`2030`$hclust_2030,              bioreg_future$nn$`2040`$hclust_2040,              bioreg_future$nn$`2050`$hclust_2050) names(future_hclt)  # 2. Compute change metrics across those four layers future_bioregDiff = dissmapr::map_bioreg_diff(future_hclt, approach = "all")  # 3. Mask to your RSA boundary (assuming 'grid_masked' is your template) mask_future_bioregDiff = terra::mask(   terra::resample(future_bioregDiff, grid_masked, method = "near"),   grid_masked )  # 4. Plot all five metrics in a 3×2 panel old_par = par(mfrow = c(3, 2), mar = c(1, 1, 1, 5)) titles = c(   "Difference count",   "Shannon entropy",   "Stability",   "Transition frequency",   "Weighted change index" ) for (i in seq_along(titles)) {   plot(     mask_future_bioregDiff[[i]],     # col      = viridisLite::turbo(100),     col      = viridis(100, direction = -1),     colNA    = NA,     axes     = FALSE,     main     = titles[i],     cex.main = 0.8   )   plot(terra::vect(rsa), add = TRUE, border = "black", lwd = 0.4) } par(old_par)

what: 1. Generate site by environment matrix using get_enviro_data()
title: Environmental data for sites
text: Spatial models informative sampling unit couples biological response (example, sampling effort species richness) suite environmental predictors.get_enviro_data() attaches environmental predictors grid cell via six-stage routine: o buffer analysis lattice, o retrieve read required rasters, o crop buffered extent, o extract raster values every grid-cell centroid, o interpolate missing data gaps, o append finished covariate set grid summary. subsections implement workflow: Download sample 19 WorldClim bioclim variables: obtains 5-arc-min (~10 km) WorldClim v2.1, returns bio stack via geodata, crops , attaches climate values every centroid. Bind climate, effort, richness one raster stack: combines √-scaled effort (obs_sum), √-scaled richness (spp_rich), 19 climate layers single SpatRast aligned 0.5° grid. Inspect extracted covariates: produces quick map (e.g. mean annual temperature) previews data verify alignment plausibility. Assemble modelling matrix: consolidates coordinates, effort, richness, climate predictors tidy data frame (grid_env) ready statistical modelling. Optional >> Reproject centroids metric-space analyses: converts centroid coordinates WGS-84 (EPSG:4326) Albers Equal-Area projection (EPSG:9822) analyses require distances metres. Download sample 19 WorldClim bioclim variables Fetch 5-arc-min (~10 km) bioclim stack via geodata package attach values every centroid. get_enviro_data() buffered grid centroids 10 km, fetched requested rasters, cropped , extracted values centroid, filled isolated NAs, merged results obs_sum spp_rich.
code: # Retrieve 19 bioclim layers (≈10-km, WorldClim v2.1) for all grid centroids data_path = "_data" # cache folder for rasters enviro_list = dissmapr::get_enviro_data(   data       = grid_spp,                  # centroids + obs_sum + spp_rich   buffer_km  = 10,                        # pad the AOI slightly   source     = "geodata",                 # WorldClim/SoilGrids interface   var        = "bio",                     # bioclim variable set   res        = 5,                         # 5-arc-min ≈ 10 km   path       = data_path,   sp_cols    = 7:ncol(grid_spp),          # ignore species columns   ext_cols   = c("obs_sum", "spp_rich")   # carry effort & richness through )  # Quick checks  str(enviro_list, max.level = 1)  # (Optional) Assign concise layer names for readability # Find names here https://www.worldclim.org/data/bioclim.html names_env = c("temp_mean","mdr","iso","temp_sea","temp_max","temp_min",               "temp_range","temp_wetQ","temp_dryQ","temp_warmQ",               "temp_coldQ","rain_mean","rain_wet","rain_dry",               "rain_sea","rain_wetQ","rain_dryQ","rain_warmQ","rain_coldQ") names(enviro_list$env_rast) = names_env  # (Optional) Promote frequently-used objects env_r = enviro_list$env_rast    # cropped climate stack env_df = enviro_list$env_df      # site × environment data-frame  # Quick checks  env_r dim(env_df); head(env_df)

what: 2. Bind climate, effort, and richness into one raster stack
title: Environmental data for sites
text: Fuse √-scaled sampling‐effort (obs_sum) richness (spp_rich) layers 19 bioclim rasters single, co-registered SpatRast. unified stack ensures predictors share grid, streamlining downstream map algebra, multivariate modelling, spatial cross-validation.
code: # --- Rebuild rasters inside the vignette --- effRich_r = sqrt(   grid_r[[c("obs_sum", "spp_rich")]] )  # Use first layer as template explicitly template = effRich_r[[1]]  env_resampled = terra::resample(   env_r,   template,   method = "bilinear" )  env_effRich_r = c(effRich_r, env_resampled)  # --- Safe plotting --- old_par = par(no.readonly = TRUE) on.exit(par(old_par), add = TRUE)  layout(matrix(1:4, nrow = 2))  titles = c(   "Sampling effort (√obs count)",   "Species richness (√unique count)",   "BIO1: Annual Mean Temperature",   "BIO2: Mean Diurnal Temperature Range" )  for (i in 1:4) {   terra::plot(     env_effRich_r[[i]],     col    = viridisLite::turbo(100),     breaks = 100,     colNA  = NA_character_,     axes   = FALSE,     main   = titles[i],     cex.main = 0.8   )   terra::plot(terra::vect(rsa), add = TRUE, border = "black", lwd = 0.4) }

what: 5. Reproject centroids for metric-space analyses** using sf::st_transform()[OPTIONAL]
title: Environmental data for sites
text: Certain analyses (e.g. spatial clustering, variogram modelling) require coordinates metres rather degrees. snippet converts centroid layer Albers Equal-Area projection. point every grid cell species metrics, climate predictors, optionally projected metre coordinates, single tidy object.
code: # Convert the centroid columns to an sf object centroids_sf = sf::st_as_sf(   grid_env,   coords = c("centroid_lon", "centroid_lat"),   crs    = 4326,          # WGS-84   remove = FALSE )  # Reproject to Albers Equal Area (EPSG 9822) centroids_aea = sf::st_transform(centroids_sf, 9822)  # Append projected X–Y back onto the data-frame grid_env = cbind(   grid_env,   sf::st_coordinates(centroids_aea) |>     as.data.frame() |>     setNames(c("x_aea", "y_aea"))   # rename within the pipeline ) names(grid_env) head(grid_env[, c("grid_id","centroid_lon","centroid_lat","x_aea","y_aea")])

what: 6. Diagnose and mitigate collinearity with rm_correlated()
title: Environmental data for sites
text: Highly inter-correlated predictors inflate variance, bias coefficient estimates, complicate ecological inference.rm_correlated() screens environmental matrix pairwise correlations exceed user-defined threshold (|r| > 0.70), iteratively prunes variable highest average absolute correlation. routine Computes Pearson (default) Correlation matrix supplied columns; Ranks variables mean absolute correlation; Discards worst offender, recomputes matrix, repeats remaining pairs lie threshold; Optional >> displays final Correlation heat-map visual QC. result reduced predictor set retains maximal information minimising multicollinearity. env_vars_reduced now contains decorrelated subset climate predictors suitable stable GLMs, GAMs, machine-learning, ordination workflows.
code: # (Optional) Rename BIO names(env_df) = c("grid_id", "centroid_lon", "centroid_lat", names_env, "obs_sum", "spp_rich")    # Run the filter and compare dimensions # Filter environmental predictors for |r| > 0.70 env_vars_reduced = dissmapr::rm_correlated(   data       = env_df[, c(4, 6:24)],  # drop ID + coord columns   cols       = NULL,                  # infer all numeric cols   threshold  = 0.70,   plot       = TRUE                   # show heat-map of retained vars )  # Before vs after c(original = ncol(env_df[, c(4, 6:24)]),   reduced  = ncol(env_vars_reduced))

title: Compute orderwise examples
text: demonstrate quantify biodiversity patterns using two common ecological metrics: species richness community turnover (beta diversity). analyses utilize compute_orderwise() function dissmapr package, applying metric functions calc_richness() calc_turnover() respectively, spatial biodiversity data organised grid_spp dataset.

previous_headings: dissmapr > Analysis of Species Richness and Community Turnover
title: Compute orderwise examples
text: calculate species richness across sites block_sp dataset, using compute_orderwise() function. calc_richness() metric function applied grid_id column site identification, species data specified sp_cols. Orders 1 4 computed .e. order=1, computes basic species richness individual sites, higher orders (2 4) represent differences richness pairwise /multi-site combinations. subset 1000 samples used higher-order computations speed-computation time. Parallel processing enabled 4 worker threads improve performance. output table summarizing species richness across specified orders. Plot order-wise richness (orders 2:5) calculated using compute_orderwise(..., func = calc_richness, ...) visualise spatial patterns richness across different orders. Results highlight regions high low richness compared across orders.
code: # Compute species richness (order 1) and the difference thereof for orders 2 to 4 rich_o1234 = dissmapr::compute_orderwise(   df = grid_spp,   func = calc_richness,   site_col = 'grid_id',   sp_cols = sp_cols,   sample_no = 1000,   order = 1:4,   parallel = FALSE,   n_workers = 4)  # Check results head(rich_o1234) # Plot species richness distribution by order boxplot(sqrt(value) ~ order,         data = rich_o1234,         col = c('#4575b4', '#99ce8f', '#fefab8', '#d73027'),         horizontal = TRUE,         outline = FALSE,         main = 'Distribution of √Species Richness by Order')  # Link centroid coordinates back to `rich_o1234` data.frame for plotting rich_o1234$centroid_lon = grid_spp$centroid_lon[match(rich_o1234$site_from, grid_spp$grid_id)] rich_o1234$centroid_lat = grid_spp$centroid_lat[match(rich_o1234$site_from, grid_spp$grid_id)]  # Summarise turnover by site (spatial location) mean_rich_o1234 = rich_o1234 %>%   group_by(order, site_from, centroid_lon, centroid_lat) %>%   summarize(value = mean(value, na.rm = TRUE))  # Check results head(mean_rich_o1234) # Plot Richness calculated using `compute_orderwise(..., func = calc_richness, ...)` ggplot() +   geom_tile(data = mean_rich_o1234[mean_rich_o1234$order==1,],             aes(x = centroid_lon, y = centroid_lat, fill = sqrt(value))) +   scale_fill_gradientn(colors = viridis(8)) + #Apply viridis color palette   geom_sf(data = rsa, fill = NA, color = "black", alpha = 0.5) +   theme_minimal() +   labs(x = "Longitude", y = "Latitude", fill = "√Species Richness") +   theme(panel.grid = element_blank(),panel.border = element_blank()   ) # Plot order-wise richness (orders 2:5) calculated using `compute_orderwise(..., func = calc_richness, ...)` ggplot() +   geom_tile(data = mean_rich_o1234, aes(x = centroid_lon, y = centroid_lat, fill = sqrt(value))) +   scale_fill_viridis_c(option = "turbo", name = "Richness") +   geom_sf(data = rsa, fill = NA, color = "black", alpha = 0.5) +   theme_minimal() +   labs(     title = "Mean √Richness by Order",     x = "Longitude",     y = "Latitude"   ) +   facet_wrap(~ order, ncol = 2)

what: 1. User-defined area of interest and grid resolution
title: User-defined grid
text: Defining geographic extent analysis grid early ensures subsequent data extraction, aggregation, visualisation tasks carried within consistent spatial framework. vignette : Load national boundary South Africa set area interest (AoI). Select working resolution 0.5° (≈ 55 km) balance spatial detail computational cost. Convert AoI terra vector raster operations run efficiently. Create blank raster template using chosen resolution AoI’s CRS (Coordinate Reference System). Populate raster placeholder values (simply 1). Mask raster AoI cells whose centroids fall within South Africa remain.
code: # 1. Load the national boundary  # The shapefile is shipped with the package for full reproducibility. rsa = sf::st_read(system.file("extdata", "rsa.shp", package = "dissmapr"))  # 2. Choose a working resolution  # A 0.5‑degree cell size strikes a balance between computational load and # the spatial resolution at which national‑level biodiversity patterns remain # interpretable. res = 0.5   # decimal degrees° (≈ 55 km at the equator)  # 3. Convert the AoI to a 'terra' vector  # 'terra' supports fast raster operations; converting now avoids repeated # coercion later. rsa_vect = terra::vect(rsa)  # 4. Initialise a blank raster template  # The template inherits the AoI’s coordinate reference system (CRS) and is # discretised into equally‑sized cells according to the resolution chosen. grid = terra::rast(rsa_vect, resolution = res, crs = terra::crs(rsa_vect))  # 5. Populate the raster with placeholder values  # We simply assign the value 1 to every cell; the values themselves are # irrelevant at this stage—the grid’s geometry is what matters. terra::values(grid) = 1  # 6. Clip the raster to the AoI  # Any cells whose centroids fall outside the boundary are set to NA, thereby # restricting subsequent computations to the AoI only. grid_masked = terra::mask(grid, rsa_vect) # grid_masked is now a 0.5° lattice clipped to South Africa and will serve as the common spatial denominator for all downstream summaries.

what: 5. Model drivers of compositional turnover with zetadiv::Zeta.msgdm()
title: Zeta diversity
text: Zeta.msgdm() extends Generalised Dissimilarity Modelling (GDM) simple pairwise similarity order ζ-diversity. Order 2 , definition, pairwise case counts species shared two sites. words, results directly comparable conventional β-diversity models. advantage ζ framework can raise order (ζ₃, ζ₄, …) function reveal higher-order patterns without changing tools. fit order-2 model ask: - strongly climate, geography, predictors control chance two sites share species? - control change along environmental gradient? Zeta.msgdm() proceeds three stages: Sampling: draws 1 000 random site pairs (sam = 1000) keep computation tractable. Normalisation: converts order-2 ζ counts Jaccard similarity (normalize = "Jaccard") coefficients range 0 1. Regression: fits -spline MSGDM (reg.type = "ispline") separates monotonic environmental effects Euclidean geographic distance (distance.type = "Euclidean"). fitted model (zeta2) contains partial -splines every predictor (note: higher splines imply stronger turnover per unit change). General Interpretation: -spline height: taller curve, variable drives species turnover. Curve shape: Steep early rises mark thresholds small environmental changes cause large compositional shifts; flatter tails suggest saturation. Distance spline: Shows residual spatial decay environmental effects removed, highlighting dispersal limits unmeasured factors. isolating driver’s effect, MSGDM pinpoints gradients erode shared biodiversity management actions effectively slow loss. Specific Interpretation: figure shows fitted -splines multi-site generalized dissimilarity model (via Zeta.msgdm), represent partial, monotonic relationship predictor community turnover (ζ-diversity) 0–1 “rescaled” range. key take-aways: Distance (blue asterisks) far largest -spline amplitude—rising ~0 zero distance ~0.05 maximum. tells us spatial separation strongest driver multi‐site turnover, even small increases distance yield substantial drop shared species. Sampling intensity (obs_sum, open circles) comes next, gentle steady rise ~0.045. indicates sites observations tend share species (, conversely, incomplete sampling can depress apparent turnover). Precipitation variables: Rain warm quarter (rain_warmQ, squares) Rain dry quarter (rain_dry, triangles-) show moderate effects (-spline heights ~0.02–0.03). means differences seasonal rainfall regimes contribute noticeably changes community composition. Temperature metrics: Mean temperature (temp_mean, triangles-), Wet‐quarter temperature (temp_wetQ, X’s), Dry‐quarter temperature (temp_dryQ, diamonds), isothermality index (iso, plus signs) low, almost flat -splines (max heights ≲0.01). words, thermal variables explain little additional turnover ’ve accounted distance rainfall. Key point: Spatial distance dominant structuring factor data .e. sites apart share markedly fewer species. accounting , differences observation effort , lesser degree, seasonal rainfall still shape multisite community similarity. Temperature seasonality metrics, contrast, appear minor independent influence zeta‐diversity landscape.
code: set.seed(123) # Fit order-2 MSGDM on the presence–absence matrix and reduced covariate set zeta2 = zetadiv::Zeta.msgdm(   grid_spp_pa[,-(1:6)],                            # species matrix (rows = sites, cols = spp)   env_vars_reduced,                                # decorrelated environmental variables   # env_vars_reduced[,-7],                         # without sampling effort included   grid_env[, c("centroid_lon", "centroid_lat")],   # longitude & latitude (°)   # grid_env[, c("x_aea", "y_aea")],               # longitude & latitude (meters)   sam           = 5000,   order         = 2,   distance.type = "Euclidean",   normalize     = "Jaccard",   reg.type      = "ispline" )  # Extract and plot the fitted I-splines # splines = Return.ispline(zeta2, env_vars_reduced[,-7], distance = TRUE) # Without sampling effort splines = Return.ispline(zeta2, env_vars_reduced, distance = TRUE) Plot.ispline(splines, distance = TRUE) # Deviance explained summary results with(summary(zeta2$model), 1 - deviance/null.deviance)  # [1] 0.3733073 # 0.3733073 means that approximately 37% of the variability in the response # variable is explained by your model. This is relatively low, suggesting that the # model may not be capturing much of the underlying pattern in the data.  # Model summary results summary(zeta2$model)

what: 6. Uneven sampling can disguise the true drivers of biodiversity
title: Zeta diversity
text: sampling effort (obs_sum) included, sites lots records suddenly look far alike poorly sampled ones, climate curves flatten, distance drops . Without sampling effort (obs_sum) temperature rainfall curves climb high fast: climate looks like main reason sites stop sharing species. Distance (blue line) still matters, less several climate variables. sampling effort removed model explains ≈ 8% deviance, compared 37%. words, discounting chance, less one-tenth variation shared-species counts captured climate distance alone, confirming survey effort primary driver much higher explanatory power full model. Key point: Uneven sampling can hide real drivers biodiversity. Without accounting effort, model attributes turnover climate. Adding effort shows well-surveyed sites appear similar simply thorough searches record species, lightly sampled sites miss many seem distinct. Correcting effort essential; can see climate distance truly shape species turnover.
code: # Fit order-2 MSGDM on the presence–absence matrix and reduced covariate set set.seed(123) # set.seed to generate exactly the same random results i.e. sam=100 zeta2_noEff = zetadiv::Zeta.msgdm(   grid_spp_pa[,-(1:6)],                            # species matrix (rows = sites, cols = spp)   # env_vars_reduced,                              # decorrelated environmental variables   env_vars_reduced[,-7],                           # without sampling effort included   grid_env[, c("centroid_lon", "centroid_lat")],   # longitude & latitude (°)   # grid_env[, c("x_aea", "y_aea")],               # longitude & latitude (meters)   sam           = 2000,   order         = 2,   distance.type = "Euclidean",   normalize     = "Jaccard",   reg.type      = "ispline" )  # Extract and plot the fitted I-splines splines_noEff = Return.ispline(zeta2_noEff, env_vars_reduced[,-7], distance = TRUE) # Without sampling effort Plot.ispline(splines_noEff, distance = TRUE) # Deviance explained summary results with(summary(zeta2_noEff$model), 1 - deviance/null.deviance)  # [1] 0.09495599 # 0.09495599 means that approximately 1% of the variability in the response # variable is explained by your model. This is relatively low, suggesting that the # model may not be capturing much of the underlying pattern in the data.  # Model summary results summary(zeta2_noEff$model)

what: 3. Plot Partial‐Dependence Curves for All Covariates
title: Zeta-MSGDM with dissmapr
text: produce unified, multi‐panel display predictor’s ‐spline partial‐dependence curve using plot_ispline_lines() helper. function : Auto‐detect spline column covariate (e.g. “dist” → dist_is). Draw thin line zeta‐order. Mark selected quantiles along raw covariate small symbols. Highlight curve’s minimum value larger marker. loop raw covariates (ending _is), generate separate plot per variable, assemble cohesive multi‐panel layout using patchwork package. makes possible compare turnover responses across full suite environmental drivers. Ecological Interpretation Conservation Implications predictors drive turnover shifts number sites: * Two‐sites: Distance dominates. Shared species drop steeply sites become farther apart. * Three‐sites: Isothermality (stable day–night vs. seasonal temperature swings) important, suggesting communities areas steady daily temperatures stay similar. * Four‐sites: Mean temperature wet‐quarter temperature strongest effects, indicating thermal limits filter species across moderate clusters sites. * Five‐sites: Sampling effort peaks influence, warning uneven survey intensity can masquerade real ecological turnover scale. * Six‐sites: Rainfall variables—especially warm‐quarter dry‐season rainfall—become key filters, showing moisture availability extreme seasons governs species overlap larger site groups. Key point: smallest scale, dispersal barriers (distance) set stage species can overlap. expand three, four sites, environmental filters—first thermal, hydric—sequentially take . scale‐dependent shift reveals different ecological processes dominate community assembly different spatial extents, direct implications design surveys target conservation changing climates.
code: # 1. Identify all raw covariates with a spline term raw_vars = sub("_is$", "",                 grep("_is$", names(ispline_tabs_all), value = TRUE))  # 2. Generate one plot per covariate plots = lapply(raw_vars, function(var) {   dissmapr::plot_ispline_lines(     ispline_data = ispline_tabs_all,     x_var        = var,     orders       = paste("Order", 2:6),     cols         = c('green','cyan','purple','blue','black'),     shapes       = c(15,16,17,18,19)   ) +   ggplot2::ggtitle(paste("I-Spline Partial Effect of", var)) })  # 3. Combine into a grid (2 columns here; adjust ncol as needed) patchwork::wrap_plots(plots, ncol = 2) +   patchwork::plot_annotation(     title = "Multi-Panel I-Spline Curves Across Covariates",     theme = ggplot2::theme(plot.title = ggplot2::element_text(size = 16, face = "bold"))   )  # # Simle single covariate line plot for "dist" # plot_ispline_lines( #   ispline_data = ispline_tabs_all, #   x_var        = "dist",   #   orders       = paste("Order", 2:6), #   cols         = c('green','cyan','purple','blue','black'), #   shapes       = c(15,16,17,18,19) # )

what: 4. Facetted boxplots of all spline terms
title: Zeta-MSGDM with dissmapr
text: Finally, summarize distribution every _is basis across orders using plot_ispline_boxplots(). spline term facetted free scales, fills mapped zOrder via color-blind–friendly Viridis palette. Ecological Interpretation Conservation Implications factors matter depends many sites compare : - Two sites: Geographic distance dominates. Nearby sites share many species, distant sites . - Three sites: Isothermality (day–night versus seasonal swings) strongest effect, suggesting stable daily temperatures support consistent communities. - Four sites: Temperature (mean seasonal highs) becomes key driver, indicating thermal limits filter species can persist across moderate clusters. - Five sites: Dry-season rainfall peaks importance, showing moisture availability determines whether species can survive across larger groups. - Four sites (): Sampling effort bias highest, meaning uneven survey intensity can look like ecological signal scale. Key points: - small scales (two sites), species must actually move locations, distance main barrier sharing species. - medium scales (three five sites), local climate steps : species can tolerate temperature moisture levels hang across multiple sites. - Breaking habitat makes even harder species move, hotter, drier conditions shrink range can survive—driving faster loss biodiversity. - Protecting connected corridors variety microclimates helps species disperse find refuge, slowing turnover preserving common “backbone” species keep ecosystems stable healthy.
code: # Facetted boxplots of all *_is columns dissmapr::plot_ispline_boxplots(   ispline_data   = ispline_tabs_all,   ispline_suffix = "_is",   order_col      = "zOrder",   palette        = "viridis",   direction      = -1,   ncol           = 3 )

code: if (requireNamespace("terra", quietly = TRUE)) {   # simulate a single data.frame   set.seed(42)   df <- data.frame(     centroid_lon = runif(100, 16, 33),     centroid_lat = runif(100, -35, -22),     pred_zetaExp = rnorm(100)   )    # single scenario clustering (use a method that does NOT require optional packages)   out1 <- map_bioreg(     data       = df,     scale_cols  = c("pred_zetaExp", "centroid_lon", "centroid_lat"),     method      = "kmeans",     k_override  = 4,     x_col       = "centroid_lon",     y_col       = "centroid_lat",     interpolate = "none",     plot        = FALSE   )   out1$methods    # simulate multiple scenarios   df2 <- df   df2$centroid_lon <- df2$centroid_lon + 5   scen_list <- list(current = df, future = df2)    out2 <- map_bioreg(     data       = scen_list,     scale_cols  = c("pred_zetaExp", "centroid_lon", "centroid_lat"),     method      = "kmeans",     k_override  = 3,     x_col       = "centroid_lon",     y_col       = "centroid_lat",     interpolate = "none",     plot        = FALSE   )   out2$methods } #> [1] "kmeans"  # Optional GMM clustering requires the 'mclust' package: # if (requireNamespace("mclust", quietly = TRUE) && requireNamespace("terra", quietly = TRUE)) { #   out_gmm <- map_bioreg(df, c("pred_zetaExp", "centroid_lon", "centroid_lat"), method = "gmm", #                         k_override = 4, x_col = "centroid_lon", y_col = "centroid_lat", #                         interpolate = "none", plot = FALSE) # }

what: Map Bioregional Change Metrics Between Categorical Raster Layers — map_bioreg_diff
title: Map Bioregional Change Metrics Between Categorical Raster Layers — map_bioreg_diff
text: Calculates five complementary indices quantify categorical (e.g. bioregion / cluster) label raster cell changes across temporal scenario stack rasters: Difference count - total number times cell's label differs first layer. Shannon entropy - information-theoretic diversity labels within cell's time-series. Stability - proportion layers label identical first layer (1 = always unchanged, 0 = always different). Transition frequency - sum binary change maps successive layers (often change occurs pair neighbours). Weighted change index - cumulative dissimilarity-weighted change weight derived empirical frequency transitions pairs labels.

title: Map Bioregional Change Metrics Between Categorical Raster Layers — map_bioreg_diff

text: dissimilarity weights weighted change index built observed transition table successive layers, normalised lie 0 1 (larger = rarer transition).  function accepts either multi-layer SpatRaster plain list single-layer SpatRasters, internally concatenated terra.

title: Map Bioregional Change Metrics Between Categorical Raster Layers — map_bioreg_diff
code: ## ------------------------------------------------------------- ## Minimal reproducible example with three random categorical ## rasters (four classes, identical geometry) ## ------------------------------------------------------------- if (requireNamespace("terra", quietly = TRUE)) {   set.seed(42)    r1 <- terra::rast(nrows = 40, ncols = 40,                     vals  = sample(1:4, 40 * 40, TRUE))   r2 <- terra::rast(r1, vals = sample(1:4, terra::ncell(r1), TRUE))   r3 <- terra::rast(r1, vals = sample(1:4, terra::ncell(r1), TRUE))    r_stack <- terra::rast(list(r1, r2, r3))   names(r_stack) <- paste0("t", 1:3)    ## 1. All five metrics   diff_all <- map_bioreg_diff(r_stack, approach = "all")   print(diff_all)    ## 2. Just the Shannon-entropy layer   ent <- map_bioreg_diff(r_stack, approach = "shannon_entropy")   terra::plot(ent, main = "Shannon entropy of label sequence") } #> class       : SpatRaster #> size        : 40, 40, 5  (nrow, ncol, nlyr) #> resolution  : 9, 4.5  (x, y) #> extent      : -180, 180, -90, 90  (xmin, xmax, ymin, ymax) #> coord. ref. : lon/lat WGS 84 (CRS84) (OGC:CRS84) #> source(s)   : memory #> names       : Differ~_Count, Shanno~ntropy, Stability, Transi~quency, Weight~_Index #> min values  :             0,            -0,         0,             0,             0 #> max values  :             2,      1.098612,         1,             2,      0.325991

title: Plot facetted boxplots for all ispline basis columns — plot_ispline_boxplots
text: Creates multi-facet boxplot every ispline basis column data, grouped specified order factor (e.g. zeta orders). spline term (columns ending _is) gets facet, uses color-blind-friendly Viridis palette.

title: Plot facetted boxplots for all ispline basis columns — plot_ispline_boxplots
text: ispline_data data frame returned run_ispline_models, containing raw covariates, spline bases (suffixed _is), order column. ispline_suffix string suffix identifying spline columns. Default “_is”. order_col name grouping column (e.g. “zOrder”). palette One Viridis options (“viridis”, “magma”, “plasma”, “cividis”, etc.). Default “viridis”. direction Integer 1 -1 control palette direction. Default -1 (reversed). ncol Number columns facet wrap. Default 3. outlier_size Size outlier points. Default 0.5.

text: ispline_data data frame returned run_ispline_models, containing raw covariates, _is spline bases, zOrder column. x_var string; name raw covariate plot (e.g. “dist”). function look matching spline column named <x_var>_is. orders Character vector zOrder levels (desired legend/order). title: Predict Pairwise Compositional Turnover (zeta-dissimilarity) with Richness — predict_dissim
text: Takes raw species environmental data, fits multi-site GDM model output, computes predicted pairwise turnover (zeta2) across landscape, returns data frame site-level richness, environmental covariates, distance, predicted turnover; optionally plots heatmap zeta2 predictions.

title: Predict Pairwise Compositional Turnover (zeta-dissimilarity) with Richness — predict_dissim
code: predict_dissim(   grid_spp,   species_cols,   env_vars,   zeta_model,   grid_xy,   bndy_fc = NULL,   x_col = "x",   y_col = "y",   show_plot = TRUE,   skip_scale = FALSE )

title: Predict Pairwise Compositional Turnover (zeta-dissimilarity) with Richness — predict_dissim
text: grid_spp Data frame containing site IDs, coordinates, species presence-absence/abundance columns. species_cols Integer character vector giving columns grid_spp hold species data. env_vars Data frame raw environmental predictors (unscaled; rows must align grid_spp). zeta_model Fitted object Zeta.msgdm (order = 2, reg.type = "ispline"). grid_xy Data frame site coordinates, row-order grid_spp, columns x_col, y_col. bndy_fc Optional sf SpatVector polygon overlay. x_col Name x (longitude) column grid_spp/grid_xy. y_col Name y (latitude) column. show_plot Logical; TRUE (default), attach turnover heatmap "plot" attribute returned data frame. Access via attr(result, "plot"). skip_scale Logical; TRUE, skip centering scaling environmental variables (default FALSE).

title: Remove Highly Correlated Predictors — rm_correlated
text: Detects pairs (groups) strongly collinear predictors eliminates minimum subset necessary keep every absolute pairwise correlation user-defined threshold.  Correlations computed stats::cor(); variables discard chosen via caret::findCorrelation() algorithm.  optional heat-map correlation matrix produced corrplot rapid inspection.

title: Remove Highly Correlated Predictors — rm_correlated

title: Remove Highly Correlated Predictors — rm_correlated
text: data frame containing original rows subset predictor columns whose absolute pairwise correlations \(<\) threshold.

title: Remove Highly Correlated Predictors — rm_correlated
text: Non-numeric columns silently dropped prior correlation calculation. cols supplied (numeric character), columns tested; otherwise numeric columns data used. names removed retained variables printed console transparency.

title: Helper functions for compute_orderwise  — utils
text: suite helper functions compute ecological indices used compute_orderwise(), including geographic distance, dissimilarity metrics, correlations, mutual information.

title: Helper functions for compute_orderwise  — utils
text: helpers written : vectorised pairwise case (two sites/vectors), safe order = 1 case (single site/vector) returning sensible scalar (often 0 NA) instead erroring. Additional packages used via explicit namespace calls: vegan (Bray-Curtis), entropy (mutual information), tibble/dplyr (pairwise matrix helpers).

title: dissmapr 0.1.0
text: Initial release dissmapr package. Added get_occurrence_data() import harmonise biodiversity occurrence records. Added format_df() convert long wide biodiversity data layouts. Added generate_grid() build spatial grids compute gridded species summaries. Added assign_mapsheet() assign mapsheet codes spatial points. Added get_enviro_data() retrieve, crop, extract environmental rasters. Added rm_correlated() remove highly correlated predictors. Added compute_orderwise() multi-order ecological metric computation. Added run_ispline_models() fit Zeta.msgdm ispline models across zeta orders. Added predict_dissim() predict pairwise compositional turnover. Added plot_ispline_lines() plot_ispline_boxplots() ispline visualisation. Added map_bioreg() clustering interpolation bioregional data. Added map_bioreg_diff() map bioregional change metrics. Added helper index functions: calc_richness(), calc_turnover(), calc_abund(), calc_phi_coef(), calc_cor_spear(), calc_cor_pears(), calc_diss_bcurt(), calc_orderwise_gower(), calc_mutual_info(), calc_geodist().
